steps.action: The ritual consists of dance and the climbing of with weight transfer through footwork, pivot turn, hold hands, partner opposite, accent beat, grounded.
steps.text: The ritual consists of dance and the climbing of with weight transfer through footwork, pivot turn, hold hands, partner opposite, accent beat, grounded.

steps.action: The fifth remains on top of the pole, dancing an
steps.text: The fifth remains on top of the pole, dancing and playing a flute and drum.

steps.action: The youths climb the pole and four jump off whil
steps.text: The youths climb the pole and four jump off while the fifth played music.

steps.action: The exact origin of this ritual/dance is unknown
steps.text: The exact origin of this ritual/dance is unknown, but it is thought to have originated with the Huastec, Nahua and Otomi peoples in Sierra de Puebla and mountain areas of Veracruz.

steps.action: The main dancer stands in the center and plays a
steps.text: The main dancer stands in the center and plays a flute, which represents the sound of birds singing.

steps.action: The four modern day voladores typically circle t
steps.text: The four modern day voladores typically circle the pole 13 times each, for a total of 52 circuits, or the number of years in the Aztec "calendar round".

steps.action: Traditionally, it has been taboo to allow women
steps.text: Traditionally, it has been taboo to allow women to become voladores but a few have become such, all of whom are in Puebla state.

steps.action: Among the Nahua and Otomi peoples, there is gene
steps.text: Among the Nahua and Otomi peoples, there is generally no dance before climbing the pole; the ceremony begins at the top.

steps.action: There is also a version where the suspended fram
steps.text: There is also a version where the suspended frame has five sides instead of four and the ritual involves six dancers and not five.

steps.action: The most traditional time to perform this versio
steps.text: The most traditional time to perform this version is on Holy Thursday at the culmination of a festival held on this day called the Huapangueada.

steps.action: Some dancers have rules to follow such as needin
steps.text: Some dancers have rules to follow such as needing to fast for one or more days before the ceremony and abstain from sexual relations so that the gods will look upon the ceremony favorably.

steps.action: Traditionally, women have been excluded from all
steps.text: Traditionally, women have been excluded from all Totonac ritual dancing.

steps.action: Even for the dance called La Maringuilla, the fe
steps.text: Even for the dance called La Maringuilla, the female protagonist is portrayed by a man.

steps.action: In Guatemala the flying pole dance, called Palo
steps.text: In Guatemala the flying pole dance, called Palo volador, is still celebrated in Joyabaj (15 August), Chichicastenango (17–23 January) and Cubulco (26 July).

steps.action: ” Today, pleasing the gods of old is still a par
steps.text: ” Today, pleasing the gods of old is still a part of the most traditional version of the ritual.

steps.action: The most traditional and lengthy version starts
steps.text: The most traditional and lengthy version starts from the selection and cutting of the tree to be used, to the final dance after all voladores have descended from the pole.

steps.action: It is believed to have originated with the Nahua
steps.text: It is believed to have originated with the Nahua, Huastec and Otomi peoples in central Mexico, and then spread throughout most of Mesoamerica.

steps.action: According to one myth, the ritual was created to
steps.text: According to one myth, the ritual was created to ask the gods to end a severe drought.

steps.action: Although the ritual did not originate with the T
steps.text: Although the ritual did not originate with the Totonac people, today it is strongly associated with them, especially those in and around Papantla in the Mexican state of Veracruz.

steps.action: The ceremony was named an Intangible cultural he
steps.text: The ceremony was named an Intangible cultural heritage by UNESCO in order to help the ritual survive and thrive in the modern world.

steps.action: The Aztecs believed that Danza de los Voladores
steps.text: The Aztecs believed that Danza de los Voladores was the symbol of their culture.

steps.action: The gods were withholding the rain because the p
steps.text: The gods were withholding the rain because the people had neglected them.

steps.action: The ceremony was created, to appease the gods an
steps.text: The ceremony was created, to appease the gods and bring back the rains.

steps.action: In some versions of the story, the ritual is cre
steps.text: In some versions of the story, the ritual is created by the old men of a village, who then chose five young men who were chaste.
